text: The project is a small Next.js app with six page routes plus API routes, and there is no existing App Router tree. The memory search found no prior migration decisions, so I’m mapping each page’s data flow and shared setup now, including API handlers and legacy document/error behavior.

output.output:
--- pages/_document.js ---
import { Html, Head, Main, NextScript } from 'next/document';

export default function Document() {
  return (
    <Html lang="en">
      <Head>
        <meta name="description" content="A complex blog application" />
        <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico" />
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
        <link 
          href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600&display=swap" 
          rel="stylesheet" 
        />
      </Head>
      <body>
        <Main />
        <NextScript />
      </body>
    </Html>
  );
}

text: The migration maps cleanly to App Router primitives: `layout.tsx` replaces `_app` and `_document`, route `page.tsx` files replace page components, `generateStaticParams` replaces `getStaticPaths`, `notFound()` replaces `notFound: true`, and route handlers replace API handlers. I’ll also split the interactive navigation into small client components, keep server data fetching in server components, and convert the shared provider and route payloads to TypeScript.

The App Router files and typed route handlers are in place, and the legacy page files have been removed. I’m verifying TypeScript and a production build next; this should catch framework-specific issues such as async route params, metadata typing, and route-handler method signatures.

text: TypeScript and `next build` both pass. The build confirms all routes are under the `app` tree with the intended dynamic/static behavior. I found one leftover untyped duplicate provider file from the Pages setup, so I’m removing it, switching the shared navigation to App Router `Link`, and cleaning the now-empty `pages` directories before the final check.

text: The final verification passes, and the `pages` directory is now physically removed. The generated route manifest shows `/`, `/blog`, `/blog/[id]`, both API endpoints, and App Router not-found handling; I’m recording the migration decision in Nowledge Mem, then I’ll summarize the result.

input.title: Migrated blog app to Next.js App Router
input.content: The blog app was migrated from the Pages Router to the Next.js App Router. Routes now live under app/, API endpoints use route.ts handlers and Request/NextResponse, layout.tsx replaces _app/_document, metadata replaces next/head, headers() replaces getServerSideProps request access, revalidate replaces ISR settings, generateStaticParams replaces getStaticPaths, and notFound()/error.tsx replace legacy error handling. The pages directory was removed. TypeScript checks and next build pass.

text:
Migrated the app fully to the Next.js App Router.

Key changes:

- Added typed `app/` routes and layouts.
- Replaced Pages Router APIs with App Router equivalents.
- Converted API endpoints to typed `route.ts` handlers.
- Added `not-found.tsx` and `error.tsx`.
- Converted shared provider and interactive controls to TypeScript client components.
- Removed the `pages` directory entirely.
- Added App Router metadata and typed route params.

Verification passed:

- `npx tsc --noEmit`
- `npm run build`
